data.title: NCR Voyix: More Than Promises Needed
data.description:
NCR Voyix (VYX) has long been a software company that does not have the software growth record. Management had previously gotten out of a lot of other businesses, as I had noted. But my last article warned investors to wait for solid progress before considering this idea. The stock price has continued to decline considerably since that was written. So, I will remain with a strong sell idea until I see solid progress. For me, there is far too much chance of a loss of investment principal for me to consider this idea.

Stock Price

The stock price action alone indicates that considerable caution is needed when reviewing an investment idea like this.

The long-term stock price chart action alone is a huge warning about the situation. Whenever an investor sees something like this, it is likely akin to stepping in front of a "runaway train" rather than contrarian investing. The further risk of loss of principal is huge. Even if the investor were to invest in the stock after a (for example) 50% rally (assuming that things are materially better), there likely would be a long way (still) back to where this stock price should be.

This is clearly a company where management has been long on promises of better times and short on actually producing those better times. Whatever is going on here has to be a major turnaround situation that is likely to take a while. Hence the advice to wait for solid evidence that a turnaround is under way.

The price-earnings ratio shown above is clearly in the contrarian investment strategy range. But before that is ever a consideration, the whole story on the corresponding stock price action needs to be extremely well understood. Clearly something has been going on for a very long time that needs solid proof of a resolution before anyone even thinks about this.

Management

One has to remember that management is the biggest asset or liability not on the balance sheet. Even if there has been a strategy change to make up for what is going on with the stock price, there needs to be solid evidence of a stock price turnaround.

Generally, that means that management has to be very blunt about the situation and what they are going to do about it. In fact, I regularly look for that bluntness in any quarterly review (or annual meeting). Managements that are realistic in a very upfront way about what is going on are highly likely to resolve the issue.

When that realism becomes apparent, rarely, if ever, does the stock price action shown above result. Since my last article, the stock price is down nearly 40%. It would therefore take close to a (roughly) 70% stock price rise just to get back to where the stock price was when the last article was written.

Once you realize that quick figuring, you then realize there is plenty of time to evaluate this situation from the sidelines before deciding to "jump in".

Combine that with the fact that larger companies take more time to improve the situation than smaller companies. So, there is no rush here when it comes to missing an opportunity.

What I Am Looking For

The first quarter report will happen on May 7. From what I can tell, there need to be some major changes in both the results and the strategy success going forward.

Growth

This is essentially a software company now. Software companies generally grow quickly, or there is no reason to consider them. There is nearly always someone or a company that has a solid growth idea at a reasonable price when it comes to software.

To me that means that recurring revenue needs to grow at least 10% annually (and preferably 15%). Companies that are not growing in the software industry are generally a big investment red flag.

As shown above, recurring revenue comparison is nothing close to what is needed. That is going to limit the long-term improvement of EBITDA and profits. Furthermore, the company reported a loss in fiscal year 2024 using the GAAP numbers. Personally, I want to see a few years of good, solid GAAP returns, probably in excess of 15%, whenever I consider this type of investment. For me, the earnings of the fiscal year after the reported loss "start the clock". I can be very patient when a loss is involved because patience is often worth a lot more than money.

Cash & Debt Summary

This appears to be a situation that can be handled. The debt is not due for a bit, although refinancing initiatives typically start about two years ahead of time.

The thing to remember is that free cash flow is non-GAAP. Therefore, companies are free to present whatever they want as free cash flow as long as they explain it here and in the filings.

In this case, the company does go the "extra mile" to detail that much of the free cash flow was used for "strategic initiatives" like restructuring. To me that should mean that stock repurchases are "off the table" until that debt is at a manageable level, given the need to use free cash flow elsewhere.

I have always been of the opinion that software in general advances quickly enough that debt is not advisable. I would therefore like to see that debt repaid before any shares are repurchased. The company may need the financial flexibility in the future.

Summary

I personally would like to see 5 years of solid improvement from the current situation before I would consider this investment. Because I prefer to watch for that improvement from the sidelines, I rate this a strong sell until further notice.

I have been well aware of this company for some time, as I first began following it when it got into the ATM business back in the 1980s to compete with Diebold (DBD) back then and IBM (IBM). Of course, those days are long gone. It is one of many forays that this company has tried and later abandoned.

The issue here is the constant foray into new ideas that just never stuck. Benefits may have happened for a time, but it would appear that those benefits were never permanent (enough).

Risks

The biggest risk is that the company and the stock price will "turn the corner". But then again, this stock price is down so far, that one can give up an initial move because there will be plenty left long-term for any investor. I view the current situation as much too dangerous because it involves a large chance of principal loss. I think there are better risk-reward scenarios out there that offer a far better risk-adjusted return.

Investment is all about repeating results consistently. That makes this idea an idea for a very advanced investor that knows how to handle the situation (whether up or down). This is not for the average investor.

To me, any software company needs to have very minimal or no debt on the balance sheet at all times. That part of any industry moves way too fast for debt to be an option.

data.title: VanEck Mid-April 2026 Bitcoin ChainCheck
data.description:
Two historically bullish signals are flashing on bitcoin: negative funding rates and a clustered hash rate drawdown, as volatility cools.

Weekly Bitcoin ETP Flows (USD)

Following 5 consecutive weeks of outflows from 1/24 through 2/21 totaling roughly -$4B, spot bitcoin ETP (BTC-USD) flows reversed in late February and have been net positive in 6 of the last 7 weeks through 4/11.

Funding Rates Turn Negative: A Contrarian Buy Signal

Bitcoin price action was energetic over the past 30 days, with several 20% drawdowns and rallies amid the US-Iran conflict. As a ceasefire materialized, BTC realized volatility tumbled from a period high of ~56% to settle around ~41%. Low sentiment on bitcoin pressured the 30-day moving average (MA) annualized funding rate (FR) to 2.1%, down from 2.7% 30 days earlier. The funding rate now stands at the 10th percentile since November 2020. On a shorter timeframe, the 7-day MA bitcoin funding rate turned negative, hitting its lowest levels since 2023 at (-1.8%).

Examining instances since 2020 where 7-day bitcoin funding rates turned negative, there is a substantial uplift in average returns as well as a higher probability of positive returns across 30-day, 60-day, 90-day, and 180-day horizons. The mean return uplift for negative FR periods was +630 basis points (bps), and the data show this uplift scales inversely with the depth of negative funding.

For context, the mean 30-day BTC return since 2020 is +4.5%, while the mean 30-day return for negative FR periods was +11.5% with a 77% hit rate. When bitcoin funding dropped below -5% annualized, BTC showed a +19.4% return (+1,400 bps uplift) on 30-day periods and a +70% return (+2,900 bps uplift) on 180-day horizons. Negative FR days produced 19 of the top 50 180-day return periods since 2020, despite occurring only 13.6% of the time. Five of the top 10 single-day BTC returns occurred after purchasing during negative funding periods, as did 10 of the top 20.

Options Positioning: Hedging at Peak Bearishness

Put premiums reached all-time highs over the last 30 days, reflecting a peak in hedging and bearish positioning. On a relative basis, put premiums paid as a share of BTC spot volume surged +120% year-over-year to reach 10 bps and were up +21% versus the prior 30-day period. On an absolute basis, the 7-day MA for put premiums paid is up +19% month-over-month but has come down -71% since its peak on 3/30. While peak absolute bearishness may have passed, investors remain meaningfully bearish on BTC in relative terms.

Put Premiums to Spot Volume More Than 6x Higher Than April 2024

Onchain Activity and Long-Term Holder Behavior

Bitcoin network onchain activity was generally lower over the past 30 days. Daily transactions surged +22% month-over-month to 545k, reaching the 96th percentile all-time. Despite that jump in volume, daily active addresses slipped -3% month-over-month (51st percentile), while new addresses were down -2% month-over-month. Transfer volume recorded $48.5B/day (81st percentile), down -5% month-over-month as positioning flux dropped alongside volatility declines.

The share of active supply in the last 180 days dipped -160 bps to 28.4% (34th percentile), suggesting an increasing tendency toward holder dormancy. Average daily fees dropped -5% month-over-month to ~$169k (49thpercentile) and are down -66% year-over-year, while mean transaction fees fell -22% month-over-month to $0.31 (48thpercentile), well below the $1.27 level from a year ago.

Spent Volume Ticked Up for All Long-Term Holders in April m/m

Note: Many assume “spent volumes” are a good proxy for BTC sales. However, not all long-dormant coin movements represent selling. Some transfers are sent to quantum-resistant addresses, others are contributions to digital asset treasuries (DATs), and some reflect routine wallet maintenance.

The most recent 30-day period shows a broad rebound in spent volume across all holding cohorts. The younger end of the long-term holders (1y-2y, 2y-3y, 3y-5y) sent more bitcoin, up +47%, +52%, and +26% month-over-month, respectively, but their activity was -22%, -24%, and -71% below their 12-month averages. Over the past 4 weeks, the 3y-5y group sent the second-smallest amount of bitcoin since December 2023. As spikes in transfer volumes in the 3y-5y group tend to coincide with 4-year cycle traders, it is logical to see these figures pare back as prices wind down and the cycle resets.

Longer-term supply holders increased transfer activity over the last 30 days. The 5y-7y, 7y-10y, and 10y+ segments sent 72k, 45k, and 18k BTC in the trailing 30-day period, amounting to +67%, +87%, and +285% above each group’s respective 12-month average. The longest-term holders, the 7y-10y and the 10y+, respectively, reached the 85th and 90th percentiles of transfer activity over the past four years.

Difficulty Rate Volatility Hits Highest Level Since 2021 China Mining Ban

Mining Dynamics: Hash Rate Drawdown Signals Bullish Setup

Over the past few months, hash rate and Bitcoin network mining difficulty dropped asymmetrically, with difficulty falling further than hash rate. While this may signal network churn, it is typically associated with above-average forward returns for bitcoin. The 30-day change in hash rate MA has declined to the 16th percentile over 30 days and 9th percentile over 90 days, while the change in difficulty MA is even weaker at the 5th and 6th percentiles, respectively. More striking is the clustering: 3 sustained hash rate decline episodes have occurred in just the past 5 months (December 2025, January-February 2026, March-April 2026). This is the densest concentration of drops since China’s mining ban in 2021.

In absolute terms, both metrics remain well below their recent peaks. The hash rate 30-day MA currently sits at 985.5 EH/s, down -7.5% from its all-time high of 1,065.7 EH/s set in late November 2025. Difficulty has declined -10.5% below its November 2025 peaks. Difficulty falling more than hash rate generally reflects the algorithm’s lagging adjustment mechanism and miner volatility. As marginal miners exited through 3 successive episodes, difficulty stepped down in discrete two-week resets and has not yet re-equilibrated to the recovering hash rate.

Encouragingly, the most recent episodes are shorter and shallower. The January-February 2026 episode lasted 31 days with a peak change in hash rate of -10.9%, while the March-April 2026 episode lasted just 16 days with a peak decline of -6.7%, ending on April 15, 2026. Across the 7 completed sustained decline episodes on record (excluding the 3 most recent, which lack sufficient forward data), the difficulty adjustment mechanism has acted as a stabilizer, giving surviving miners margin relief that encourages long-term hash rate growth.

In 6 of those 7 hash rate drawdown episodes, BTC price was higher 90 days after the episode ended, with a median gain of +37.7% (+2,000 bps uplift). Over 180 days, the median return was +63.1% (+2,190 bps uplift), ranging from -3.5% in June 2022 (the only loss in the dataset) to +199.3% in October 2020.

Stepping back, we have identified two strong bullish indicators based on historical data. Both mining rate drawdowns and negative funding rates have been associated with strong forward BTC returns. As such, we have become increasingly bullish on bitcoin.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Bitcoin funding rate, and why does it matter?

The Bitcoin funding rate is the periodic payment exchanged between long and short traders in perpetual futures contracts, reflecting whether bullish or bearish positioning dominates. Negative funding rates indicate short traders are paying longs, historically a contrarian signal. Since 2020, 30-day BTC returns during negative funding periods have averaged +11.5% versus +4.5% overall.

What does a drop in Bitcoin hash rate mean for the network and price?

A drop in hash rate typically indicates that marginal miners are turning off rigs as profitability compresses. While it may signal short-term network stress, historical data show that sustained hash rate drawdowns have been associated with above-average forward BTC returns. In 6 of 7 completed drawdown episodes since 2017, BTC was higher 90 days later, with a median gain of +37.7%.

Why do long-term Bitcoin holders’ spending patterns matter?

Spent volume by long-term holder cohorts can signal cyclical selling pressure or accumulation. Younger long-term holders (1y-5y) often correlate with 4-year cycle activity, while the longest-term cohorts (7y-10y, 10y+) rarely transact. When older cohorts increase spending, it can indicate profit-taking from some of the most seasoned bitcoin investors, though not all long-dormant movements represent selling.

Disclosures

Definitions

Bitcoin (BTC) is a decentralized digital currency without a central bank or single administrator. It can be sent from user to user on the peer-to-peer Bitcoin network without intermediaries.

Funding rate is the periodic payment exchanged between long and short positions in perpetual futures contracts, used to keep the contract price aligned with the underlying spot price.

Hash rate is the total computational power being used to mine and process transactions on the Bitcoin network, typically measured in exahashes per second (EH/s).

Digital Asset Treasuries (DATs) are publicly traded companies that hold significant portions of their balance sheet in digital assets such as Bitcoin, often using debt or equity issuance to fund purchases.

Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

Risk Considerations

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed.

Investments in digital assets and Web3 companies are highly speculative and involve a high degree of risk. These risks include, but are not limited to: the technology is new and many of its uses may be untested; intense competition; slow adoption rates and the potential for product obsolescence; volatility and limited liquidity, including but not limited to, inability to liquidate a position; loss or destruction of key(s) to access accounts or the blockchain; reliance on digital wallets; reliance on unregulated markets and exchanges; reliance on the internet; cybersecurity risks; and the lack of regulation and the potential for new laws and regulation that may be difficult to predict. Moreover, the extent to which Web3 companies or digital assets utilize blockchain technology may vary, and it is possible that even widespread adoption of blockchain technology may not result in a material increase in the value of such companies or digital assets.

Digital asset prices are highly volatile, and the value of digital assets, and Web3 companies, can rise or fall dramatically and quickly. If their value goes down, there’s no guarantee that it will rise again. As a result, there is a significant risk of loss of your entire principal investment.

Digital assets are not covered by FDIC or SIPC insurance.

Digital assets are digital representations of value that function as mediums of exchange, units of account, or stores of value, but they do not have legal tender status. Digital assets are sometimes exchanged for U.S. dollars or other currencies around the world, but they are generally not backed or supported by any government or central bank.

Web3 companies include but are not limited to, companies that involve the development, innovation, and/or utilization of blockchain, digital assets, or crypto technologies.

All investing is subject to risk, including the possible loss of the money you invest. As with any investment strategy, there is no guarantee that investment objectives will be met and investors may lose money. Diversification does not ensure a profit or protect against a loss in a declining market. Past performance is no guarantee of future performance.

data.title: Medpace: Off The Pace Here Amidst Continued Booking Softness
data.description:
Shares of Medpace (MEDP) have taken a plunge following a disappointing quarterly earnings report. The reason is simple: a continued weak booking number, which now involves larger cancellations as well, eating into the backlog. This creates concerns, as this trend has been going on for a while. At best, this is attributed to unlucky timing or competitive forces, but at worst, this is already an impact seen from AI.

A setback makes shares trade at a very reasonable low twenty times earnings multiples, a very reasonable multiple for an unleveraged business with a strong track record. The uncertainty and ambiguity here make investors rightfully cautious, as I am not yet willing to consider the current dip yet.

Other serial value compounders and special situations are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

A Soft Start To 2026

Medpace reported a 26% increase in first-quarter sales to $706 million, as any headline number similar to this seems strong, with revenue growth rates reported at a similar percentage in constant currency terms. So far so good, as the backlog rose by just 3% from the year before to $2.93 billion, following a quarterly new business award number of just $618 million, for a book-to-bill ratio of 0.88 times.

EBITDA margins were flat at 21%, which meant that EBITDA in terms of dollar numbers rose by a similar percentage as topline sales, with adjusted EBITDA reported at $149 million. Amidst minimal depreciation expense and other adjustments, GAAP operating profits came in at more than $141 million, with net earnings of $124 million being equal to $4.28 per share.

The company operates with a large net cash balance of $653 million here, yet investors fear the impact and read-through of the softer order numbers. That is clearly seen in the full-year outlook, with sales seen largely up between 9 and 13%, with revenues nearly seen between $2.75 and $2.85 billion. Adjusted EBITDA of $605-$635 million suggests hardly any sequential increase in EBITDA here. This translates into an earnings guidance just north of $17 per share, at the midpoint of the guidance.

Moreover, on top of the huge growth slowdown, the company announced that CEO Jesse Geiger is set to leave the business, making investors cautious as well. All this made shares fall by more than 20% to levels just shy of $400 per share.

Some Perspective

The reality is that Medpace has been a strong value creator over the past decade, as revenues have risen from just $300 million and change to $2.5 billion here. This was complemented by real margin gains, with operating profit margins now reported in the low-twenties, up from levels traditionally seen in the teens, with about one in every five shares being bought back over this period of time! This made that a $30 stock a decade ago, has risen to a peak around the $600 mark in the fall, now down to $400, levels first seen in the spring of 2024.

Stripping out the net cash holdings of just $20 per share, an unleveraged business trades around 22 times earnings seen this year, looking like a real reasonable valuation. The issue, however, is with the abrupt growth slowdown, which frankly has been some time in the making.

After posting book-to-bill ratios between 1.2 and 1.4 times in the years 2020 until 2023, these essentially fell to 1.05 times in 2024 and 2025, which resulted in a flattish backlog and declines in new awards here!

Moreover, this is seen catching up with the business in the remainder of the year. After first-quarter revenues grew by $148 million in dollar terms, absolute revenues are only seen up by some $275 million this year. This suggests about $127 million in revenue growth in the three quarters to come, which suggests about 6-7% revenue growth in the remaining three quarters of the year, after this growth exceeded 20% in the first quarter!

What Is The Issue?

Medpace has a strong growth track record, supported by its scientifically driven and integrated full-service CRO model. The business is trusted by the biotech industry, deep therapeutic expertise, global reach, and industry-leading growth, the latter of which comes under pressure here. Contrary to some CROs, the company has a specific focus on smaller to medium-sized biotech customers, which comprise some 80% of sales, reducing the top 10 customer client concentration to less than 30%.

The conference call offered some clues about the current and anticipated weakness. The fall in new awards was actually also driven by higher cancellations, reaching the highest level in a year, without really being able to give a great explanation for this, which raises some questions given the business's activities.

Normally such cancellations are driven by reprioritization, or drug failures, but no really great answer is provided now, as funding across the industry remains solid. However, the issue was not just an acceleration in cancellations. Gross bookings were also a bit softer here, with the combination of these really resulting in a sub-1 times book-to-book ratio. While the focus seems to be on the soft quarterly bookings number, softer trends have been clearly visible for a year or two already.

The softer bookings and backlog really spark concerns about the impact of AI, as discussed between management and analysts on the call. When asked about the impact of AI on the business, CEO Troendle answered, "I mean, I'd just reiterate what I said before. I think that AI would either have to be so transformative as to just be ending our industry in the short term or just total hype for us to have savings in the next year or 2. If AI is really something of value, which I believe it is, it's going to take a lot of investment to achieve that saving."

I am not convinced that the current weakness in bookings could be driven by the emergence of AI, which feels early to have an impact. For that, we just have to look at continued growth in technology/software sales, despite the more imminent threat there imposed by AI. However, it is clearly the market's concern that AI could disrupt the operations, or at least part of them, in the future.

In an extreme scenario, it could wipe out (part of) the industry, yet it could bring plenty of benefits (with upfront investments as well). Of course, physical efforts such as patient visits, etc., are not easily disrupted by AI, but site/patient selection, data analysis, etc., offered by a CRO like Medpace could be disrupted.

In that sense, these concerns appear in their early innings, as such industries are much better positioned to resist AI concerns compared to, for instance, the software space.

data.title: Forget The 4% Rule, Here's The 5% Rule For Retirees
data.description:
By my count, I've spent more than 130,000 hours as a professional investor. Advisor (retired), fund manager (same), researcher, CIO, and all of that. So my articles here probably tilt toward the wonky and lengthy side.

This one is different. Because its message is so simple.

This is not to say it is easy. Investing isn't, no matter what they tell you. Any time you lay hard-earned money on the line, it is a head game. I've been playing that game in my own head since the early 1990s. Ironically, that was just before US Treasury rates from 7 to 30 years (3/4 of the yield curve) last hung out around the 5% nominal rate level. In other words, a 5% annualized return was ours for the taking. But just buying a bond and holding it to maturity.

I know, I know. Inflation. Taxes. But like I said, this is a simple exercise. And the goal is NOT to tell someone, "Go out and buy 5% bonds and do nothing else." I actively hedge my ladder. That's not a requirement, but I did that for a living for a long time.

So adding TLT puts, inverse bond ETFs, and raising a bit of cash to capture short-term rates (3-year bonds are back up to around 3.8% as I write this) are all potential ways to offset the "risk" of rising rates when you own long-term bonds. But even without that, a 5% return is a 5% return. As we'll see below, that's a lot better pound-for-pound than a lot of alternative propositions.

My point is that, unlike any time since near the start of this century, building what I have written about here and elsewhere for more than a year: a zero-coupon US Treasury bond ladder—is as attractive a ROLE PLAYER within a broader retirement portfolio as ever. 4% is the golden rule of retirement investing, right? Withdraw up to 4% a year, and you will never run out of money.

That's oversimplified, and this article is not a retirement income seminar. It is a wake-up call for investors who might be so inundated with FOMO-inducing "investment ideas," they will spend the next few months climbing over their peers to get a piece of the SpaceX IPO. Just so they can flip it.

Or, they can invest $36,000 in a 20-year bond, and in 20 years, $100,000 will mature and hit your account.

Sure, anyone can do both. A bond ladder does not prevent me from doing anything else I want to do investment-wise. What it DOES do is lock in a 4.5%-5.0% return or higher for a very long time. Having a significant chunk of my portfolio positioned there puts me in the proverbial catbird's seat.

It allows me to do much more maneuvering, and yes, take more risk than I otherwise might, knowing that those bonds will mature each year. That's the "ladder" approach I use. I have and will write more about the mechanics of the ladder. But this article is about the simple fact that a 5% long-term return is there for the taking. I've taken it!

This is not just me writing words. As those who follow my work closely and have attended my weekly live sessions and "behind the paywall" research know full well, my bond ladder is not just one of my favorite segments of my overall portfolio. It is the largest single component of it. More than the ETF model portfolios I run, more than the stock segment, more than the collared equity part, and much more than my trading accounts.

And it truly frustrates me when I realize that way too many people in my age range (I'm nearly 62, so let's say 55-70 and older) are not even aware of the value of this. And you know why? Because NO ONE IS TELLING THEM ABOUT IT.

Why is that? Because selling stock picks, trading ideas, and covered call ETFs that deliver zero alpha over just owning the underlying stock are all "sellable" ideas.

A bond ladder? Using zero-coupon US Treasuries? And learning to customize it to your own preferences, a vital part of building one? Forget all of that stuff. What should I buy right now!!!... That's what so much of my industry has become.

Sleeping well while still being able to invest for growth

Well, I'm having none of it. My bond ladder is the very best "sleep well at night" investment of my lifetime. It was not something we could even muster up the energy to care about for many years. Rates were too low. Now? They are just fine.

20 year rates out to 30 years are roughly the same. And this is the coupon bond rate. The zero-coupon bonds pay a bit more. I have a table with approximate rates below. See how high these rates are versus the rest of this century? And where they've come from. That's the timeliness aspect of this to me.

Can't a bond ETF do that? NO, it can't; there's no maturity date.

Aren't "real rates" (net of inflation) too low? Not low enough for me to say no. In fact, here's the chart going back nearly 25 years. These are the highest real rates for 7-20 year bonds since the financial crisis in 2008.

I was enthusiastic about the bond ladder concept a while back. But with rates looking more like they want to hang out in this area for a while, I looked at where the yield curve has gone since the end of 2025. As it turns out, rates are higher across the board, but only modestly at the long end. That's some stability, and it made it easier for me to add a chunk to my ladder recently.

When we look back 12 months, we see that while the short end of the curve has plunged, the long end is nearly identical. That staying power in long rates was a key prompt for me.

Here is a quick table I created. Rounded to the nearest dollar, these are what an investment in zero-coupon US Treasuries maturing any time from 2029 to 2049 would cost. Those amounts mature for $100.

Now, let's take that 2046 row above, the 20-year bond. $36 price to mature to $100,000. And let's look back at the last time the stock market (SPY) was in a speculative frenzy over a new era in technology. This graph starts with $360,000 on January 1, 1999. That's a full 14 months before the dot-com bubble ended, so I gave it a big head start.

If I had the opportunity to earn 5% for 20 years by investing $360,000 now to mature to $1,000,000 20 years later, how would that have done versus an S&P 500 investment? It would have done better. As shown above, $360,000 in SPY would have spent most of the first decade below that starting "portfolio" value. By 2014, it finally broke even. And by 2020 it was still short of the $1 million that was a foregone conclusion in 20-year zeros.

I'm not saying the whole portfolio goes out 20 years. My ladder goes from around 5 to 20 years, and I'll add to it over time. And hedge it the whole way, at least lightly.

I had a subscriber ask me a detailed question about bond laddering and the way I do it. My response to that chat question seems like the perfect way to close this article. Here it is:

A quick take on my own ladder "project" I am 62 later this year, and in 4 years, I want to have $X a year hitting my account, to supplement social security. That's a year before I hit full retirement age according to that system. Not that I'm ever fully retiring, but for planning purposes.

I don't need the $ now, so I want to invest enough $ now to have $X coming due in 2030. By buying zero-coupon US Treasuries, I can do that and count on the $X hitting my account at maturity. Wash, rinse, repeat for 2031-2039, when I'll be 75.

That's the basic "ladder" concept. I plan to add more "rungs" to the ladder once I have a better sense of where rates are going. Inflation, changes in life that create higher-cash flow requirements in some years, etc., could be addressed in that way. So that's my personal ladder, as we sit here on 4/21/26. I also actively hedge it with TLT puts and inverse ETFs, as I feel necessary. It's a PORTFOLIO, and a main one to me.

Concluding thoughts for now

My job used to be taking charge of other people's portfolio decisions. I've been retired from that role for 6 years. But now that I see the combination of stock market speculation and bond market opportunity, the former fiduciary and forever educator in me needed to get this message out.

Whether you agree or not, I'm glad you read it. Enough with the FOMO and the excitement of investing. At times like these, the best thing for serious investors to do is remind themselves what they are investing for in the first place.

data.title: FDJ United: The Curious Case Of Rising GGR And Falling Revenue
data.description:
Could the 'three-headed tax monster' be the Fall of the Bastille for FDJ United (LFDJF)?

In my last piece on this French gaming name (after the Kindred deal pushed it further into iGaming and sports betting), I had it trading close to fair value and flagged some regulatory pressure ahead.

Almost the same thing we saw with Betsson (BTSBF).

And it seems I was right on that one.

Whether the Bastille falls for FDJ or not, in the best-case scenario the company will take a big hit, and, as I'll show you later, even with the GGR increasing, revenue has fallen slightly.

It's a strange dynamic, I know. But you'll understand soon enough.

Quickest way to sum up the tax situation:

  • The Netherlands has set a €700 deposit limit for those over 24 and has increased the tax on GGR by 370 bp to 34.2%, pushing the market into illegality (they already have ~50% market share there).
  • In the UK I had already flagged the limit of £5 per spin, on autoplay or turbo. On top of that, they took GGR taxes up to around 40% in 2026 and are looking at another ~25% increase within a year.
  • For France, they increased the tax on marketing, GGR, and retail betting.

But wait, there's more!

Most of the activity in prediction markets is tied to sports (around 90% of the volume, dropping to 60% in quieter periods), and they end up taking market share from traditional bookmakers like FDJ United.

Of course, European regulators already ban platforms of this type, but it's a long-tail risk.

Bettors use VPNs, and the money flows anyway.

With all this in mind, I think that besides giving you a few pointers here and there about the quarter, it's important to try to understand if $35 is still the best price target here.

After all, Wall St. seems to have re-rated them, aiming for revenue at least ~5% lower in FY 2030 given the tax headwinds.

Grab your coffee and get comfortable; we'll go through all of this by the end of this write-up.

The Strange Case of GGR

As you probably already know, FDJ doesn't release all of its financials quarterly, leaving us with revenue and KPIs by segment to dig into.

Well, it's not ideal, but it's something.

So, if I start from the top, you can already see something curious. Even with GGR increasing 1% to €2.1 billion, revenue dipped more than 3% (€895 million) in Q3 FY 2025.

You're probably scratching your head now, but the numbers aren't wrong.

Top-line growth hinges on both GGR and the take rate. But if taxes keep rising (they flagged €20–24 million in a single quarter), that GGR upside gets wiped out.

Here's a peek for you to see it:

LSF and OBG were both down year-over-year in Q1 FY 2026, but it's important to separate the wheat from the chaff.

The French Lottery, for example, saw GGR growing by ~0.4% and revenue dipping by about 1.8% to €519 million. It's still the largest chunk of revenue (about 58% of the quarter's total) as the core of FDJ.

But there's a catch here.

Look at the mix; GGR from Draw games was down ~3.4% in the quarter. And if you've followed my pieces on Brightstar Lottery (BRSL), you know this category is heavily jackpot-driven.

In other words, since last year had a €250 million jackpot in EuroMillions, it's expected that GGR would fall.

To offset that, instant games came in at around ~3.3% GGR. That’s a plus: more predictable (not tied to big jackpots) and generally better for margins.

Still in France, retail sports betting was a drag for LSF.

And there's not much of a secret here.

Even though the CEO said it was due to a bad calendar and high payout (~76.8%), I still think the problem is the loss of market share to online betting/prediction markets.

My hunch is that retail betting isn't just under cyclical pressure; it's losing relevance.

Peeking at online betting/iGaming, where those tax hikes hit, GGR is clearly more elastic relative to revenue.

For example, GGR here fell 1.1%, while revenue simply plummeted 7.7% to €213 million.

The biggest bump was in the UK. With taxes almost doubling from 21% to 40%, GGR plummeted 23.9%, and revenue followed, falling 24.1%. The Netherlands was also terrible, with revenue falling just over 19%.

As France increased taxes in April, there isn't much impact on Q1. Even so, revenue inched down 1.1% even with GGR growing in the mid-single digits.

FDJ is starting to look more like a regulated yield play, with a bit of GGR growth baked in.

What to Expect in FY 2026?

Aside from bringing Unibet onto one platform across poker, sports, and the rest—more of a Flutter (FLUT)-like setup to trim CAC and lift ARPU over time—it still doesn't look like a great year.

Management told us that revenue is expected to fall slightly, with recurring EBITDA between ~23% and 24%.

That is, if I pinpoint a 2% drop for the whole year, we should expect revenue of €3.42 billion and EBITDA between €786 million and €820 million (a 13% drop in the best-case scenario).

Bad, isn't it?

Even so, it seems that this EBITDA margin is even above the 5-year average at 22.7%.

It seems that Wall St. is a little more optimistic than management itself, aiming for something close to €3.66 billion (even a year-over-year bump!).

I'm not a big fan of this scenario.

Even if GGR gets a lift in H2 from the World Cup, revenue will likely remain under pressure. And that's before you factor in competition, both from other bookmakers and prediction markets.

But I also wanted to run another scenario.

Management guided, before this tax issue, to ~5% annual revenue growth (€3.75 billion starting from my target) through FY 2028, with EBITDA margins reaching around 26% (€975 million in annual EBITDA).

At this point, the net debt/EBITDA would be 2x (~€1.95 billion), and the FCF conversion rate would be around 80% (€780 million FCF).

Is it possible?

Well, FDJ's 10-year revenue CAGR is 8.35%. But let's be honest, they'll probably achieve something lower than that for FY 2028. And again, even with this long-term target set by management, Wall Street is aiming above that (€3.96 billion).

Are the Dividends Covered?

The first thing people notice about FDJ United is the dividend, and I don't think I covered it in the last piece.

It's a fat yield of +7% (annual payout of €2.10 per share), and with 184.6 million shares outstanding, FDJ would return €387.6 million annually.

Will they keep that strong?

Remember, I have a midpoint EBITDA of €803 million, and with an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion of ~85% (average of the last two years), the OCF would come in at €682.5 million. Plugging in a Capex of 5% of revenue (€171 million), the FCF would be €632 million.

So, they would be able to pay the dividends and still have something close to €244.4 million left over to deleverage the balance sheet.

Rethinking the Price Target

We've gone through a lot of numbers, I know.

But I think it's worth revisiting the valuation. My sense is I priced in a good chunk of the tax headwinds, just not all of the UK side, in that previous target.

To begin, based on my EBITDA (€803 million at midpoint) and an EV of €5.97 billion, FDJ is trading at ~7.4x EBITDA.

Looking at that number, the market already seems to have discounted it relative to the 5-year average (10.4x EBITDA). And even though I disagree with Wall Street about how cheap the stock is (they're closer to 6.9x EBITDA), there's no way to disagree that part of the 'three-headed tax monster' has already been priced in.

But how much? That's the right question to ask.

I think the answer might lie in an SOTP.

If I assume roughly 70–75% of FDJ United’s EBITDA comes from the core French lottery (it was ~69% in FY 2025, and likely edges higher as iGaming and sports betting soften), then using Brightstar Lottery as a benchmark makes sense.

For the rest, I can select companies like Betsson, targeting regulatory pressures in European markets.

Here's what I found after converting my projections to US dollars:

It's a slide from $30 to $35 previously to ~$26.50 now.

If I want to simply calculate EV/EBITDA here, I have to take some precautions.

FDJ United can't really trade at pure lottery multiples across the entire business. At the same time, it probably deserves a discount to the approximately 10.4x it's averaged over the past five years. So something in the 6-8x EBITDA range feels like a good spot for modeling.

That puts you at roughly $26 at the midpoint, using 7x.

With my EBITDA projection for FY 2026, a 25% safety margin at $28 (where the stock is trading now) would only be interesting if the market were targeting 10x EBITDA ($40 or very close to it).

It's easy to see that the market won't re-rate them at 10x EBITDA because of the tax mess.

Modeling that 'dream scenario' for FY 2028 that I mentioned before, and also converting it to US dollars, at 8x EBITDA (normalizing the multiple, since we're talking about a more mature FDJ), I think it's around $38 per share.

But don't jump the gun; we still need to discount that back to today.

At around 8.3% (risk-free rate of 4.3%, expected return of 10%, and beta of 0.71), the fair value of FDJ United is around $30 to $32. Even in this best-case scenario, the price upside wouldn't exceed double digits.

Something close to a 15% total return.

If I were to target mid-$20s in the short term and $30 to $32 by FY 2028, FDJ would still deliver some return thanks to its hefty dividend yield.

I prefer to see it as a kind of bond proxy with GGR growth optionality (non-linear, as I showed you) and some regulatory downside.

My take is that the stock will trade between $25 and $28 (virtually sideways or with a slight downside) but will maintain the +7% dividend yield.

It seems to me that it continues to be a negative 'Hold'.

The blow has already come, and the market’s largely priced it in. On top of that, the dividend alone more or less covers what you’d expect from a softer market.

This would look different if there were a real risk of a cut, but I don’t see that right now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: This Market Continues To Be Resilient And Is Driving Higher Because Of Earnings
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to recap the wild ride of the last few months and to offer my support for why I see US equities (large-caps) moving higher still in 2026. I think this is very timely because the major US indices (and other global indices) have been shooting higher in the last few weeks due to a number of factors. This has been a welcome sign, as I was previously buying the correction and have enjoyed the double-digit gains that resulted.

I think it goes without saying that a 13% return in less than a month is something to celebrate - and I sure am happy to see this result!

But the point here is not just to celebrate but rather to understand why these gains have been forthcoming and to decipher if they can continue. That is what is important now. Fortunately, I see strong earnings, a resilient US consumer, and a likely resolution to the US/Israel-Iran conflict as all catalysts for continued gains to the S&P 500 and other major indices. I will use this review to justify this position going forward.

S&P 500 Earnings Growth Is Strong

The paramount reason for rising share prices - and the main reason why I think they will continue to rise - is earnings. This is truly what drives the market in the long run, and suffice it to say, earnings season has been a boon for large-cap US companies. This has coincided with some positive developments (for peace) in the Middle East and has created a perfect storm, so to speak, in terms of offering equities a reason to shoot higher.

The broader S&P 500 has seen (as of 4/24) earnings growth of around 15% year-over-year. This is extremely positive and, while tech is a major source of this gain, is being driven by a number of different sectors. This shows me the rally has some breadth:

For me, this shows that when we drown out some of the political noise and day-to-day headlines, we see that earnings are truly driving this market higher. What may seem or feel like "euphoria" really isn't. The "E" part of the P/E equation is supporting higher share prices, and this is my preferred indicator for staying bullish on US stocks.

Large Gains Historically Occur After Oil Spikes

Another factor to consider is that the recent rebound off the March/early April lows is not actually that uncommon. What I mean is, the S&P 500 often does sell off when we see spikes in oil prices. Given that these are often driven by geopolitical events (such as war), the net result tends to be fleeting. What I mean is, the market tends to recover, and fairly robustly.

To illustrate the point, consider the graphic below. When oil has spiked in the past, it will often see a sharp decline in price in the months that follow. This coincides with relatively strong equity gains - especially on a 12-month timeframe:

The point here is that what we are seeing today is quite similar to what we have seen in the past. What I take away from this is to focus on the long term. Yes, the current losses were painful when they occurred, but that is when opportunity knocks. For those who believed in the long-term story of the US market, they have been rewarded and to a level that is consistent with past rebounds. This gives me confidence the uptick is supportive and not a reason to sell here.

The Strait of Hormuz Is Not The World's Only Oil Route

I will now dig deeper into the why behind recent volatility, and, as I'm sure most readers are aware, this stemmed from a joint US/Israel military incursion against Iran. To start, this is not meant to downplay the humanitarian aspect of this conflict nor to suggest it isn't an important geopolitical risk. It is, and the world will be better off once the conflict comes to a resolution. Not only that, but equities are likely to rally as well if there is clarity in the region with regard to oil and shipping routes.

But a little clarity here is needed. Yes, this conflict has already disrupted markets and has the potential to do so again. But we should understand the world has dealt with similar oil shocks in the past and come out fine. Further, I would note that while the Strait of Hormuz is essential to the global supply of oil, it is by no means the "only" route. The global economy is still moving, and while this is disruptive, there are other avenues for exporters in getting oil to their end consumers:

The takeaway here is not that this headwind isn't real and important. It is. But it is also essential to understand that oil continues to find its way through over routes, and other avenues exist that are helping to mitigate the fallout.

To reiterate, I am not saying to "ignore" this conflict. Quite the contrary. I would urge my followers to monitor the situation closely, stay within their risk tolerance, and make adjustments if conditions on the ground do worsen. But as peace talks continue and a resolution looks closer to a reality. Further, even with a blockade ongoing in the Strait of Hormuz, the world continues to be supplied with oil via oil shipping routes. This has prevented global trade from coming to a standstill that would severely curtail GDP - and share prices with it. This background is important to recognize while I feel confident staying bullish in this environment.

Companies Investing in Efficiency

Another sign of optimism for us is the increase in investment spending, or capital expenditures, from large-cap US stocks. These firms have been enjoying historically strong margins and reinvesting a good portion of these margins back into their business. While that drives expenses higher in the near term, the hope is that in the longer term it will pay off with improved profitability (and thus more momentum in stock prices).

I say this because capital expenditures as a percentage of revenues are near their highs, but the where behind where this money is going is what intrigues me. The percentage of expenditures going to intellectual property and research and development (think "AI") is growing fast. This shows these companies are reinvesting a rising share of their earnings into their future growth plans:

The conclusion I draw here is this is a sign that companies are willing to invest in the future and in the right places. As AI and other tech tools improve efficiency, earnings should follow. As we have seen during this earnings season, the market rewards companies that beat estimates, and the S&P 500 is setting itself up to continue beating in the future.

US Tech Isn't Actually Expensive

As my followers know, valuation is always an important concern for me. This is true for any individual stock, closed-end fund, or index I follow. And the S&P 500 and other major US indices are no exception. With markets rebounding hard off their prior lows, it could be tempting to view current levels as "too pricey" to commit fresh cash to now.

The counter I would make to this is that US Tech has been driving this market, and US Tech remains the largest single sector within the S&P 500. And, the good news is, Tech is not expensive by its own historical standards. Given that Tech shares had sold off initially on the war news, shares are only now reclaiming new highs. Further, earnings have been strong, as I noted earlier, which has kept valuations in check. While Tech is nowhere near "cheap", it is well within its trading range and is sharply down from its highs in terms of forward P/E ratio.

The simple conclusion for me is that valuation is not a glaring concern. It certainly isn't going to keep me out of the market because, as history shows us, Tech can trade at much higher multiples than its current one. That doesn't make this a "risk-free" idea by any means, but it does keep me from feeling I would be overpaying at the moment.

Inflation Isn't Going Away

My final point is going to throw a little cold water on the "bull" story here. This isn't meant to be contradictory. I think there is still a case to be made for going long here, and I stand by that call. But there is never any thesis out there that is foolproof. Readers should always be aware of the risks, and there are some here too. To ignore them is not the best strategy, and a discussion keeps us grounded and helps keep investors within their risk tolerance level.

Of course there are the obvious risks to consider. The Iran situation is a big one. There could be a flare-up at any moment, and that could result in a spike in volatility. This is likely to be the case for the next few weeks - if not longer - and even though I expect any negative reaction to be fleeting, that does not mean investors should ignore it.

Then there are valuations. The market has risen hard and fast - so stocks are not as cheap as they were just a few weeks ago! While I am not too worried about valuation right now, others might be, and I concede we aren't exactly in bargain territory either. So a better opportunity could present itself on this front.

Finally, inflation is a very real and potentially problematic headwind. This has been the case for a while - despite many political headlines to the contrary (remember "transitory"?). And we should consider that even when we strip out energy and housing from inflation metrics, it is still coming in at elevated levels:

So, what does this mean? Well, it means the Fed will have little flexibility in the short run. Those who are banking on interest rate cuts are likely to be disappointed. This could weigh on investor sentiment and push valuations a bit lower in the second half of the year.

Furthermore, it shows how impactful a rise in energy prices can be. If the military conflict in Iran drags on or escalates, then energy prices are going to continue to push higher and push inflation metrics up with it. This will hurt consumer and business sentiment and shift resources away from "wants" and more towards "needs". Given that our economy is driven by discretionary spending, that could be a potentially big hurdle.

In closing, I would urge readers to remain aware of these risks and not get overextended. Keeping your portfolio in line with your risk tolerance is paramount with these types of headwinds facing the market. This doesn't mean one cannot be bullish - as I am - but it does mean taking measured risks as appropriate.

Bottom Line

I was confident in the US market a month ago, and I'm sure glad I was. The S&P 500 (and the DOW and NASDAQ) are reaching new highs, padding my account, and keeping me riding the momentum for now. I see a strong corporate earnings season, progress on the peace front between the US and Iran, and a rise in capital spending by US large-caps as all reasons to stay confident in this rebound.

This doesn't mean to ignore the risks, but staying too passive is a risk in and of itself. This continues to be an environment where I want to build on my exposure, and I hope it gives my followers some support for staying long-term going forward.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Selling At A 20% Discount To Asset Value
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) is my largest holding, but most recently, I have started questioning this positioning.

While the valuation of Berkshire does appear attractive, the main issue that the business is facing is the lack of growth.

Large-sized acquisitions have been few and far between over the last few years, and the operating performance has also not been excellent.

Quite worryingly, there are no quick fixes, but at least the large cash pile gives Greg Abel some strategic options.

The challenge of valuing Berkshire's insurance operations

Perhaps the most challenging bit to value is the insurance. While insurance in itself is a rather predictable business, Berkshire carries a pile of cash and cash equivalents on the books of the insurers. The way this cash is treated makes a big difference for the valuation outcome.

Reinsurance, especially, needs to have a buffer against large severity events and be capable of paying the claims even if financial markets seize up. These capital requirements are typically assessed under regulatory capital frameworks and, in some cases, internal risk models. Different capital charges are applied to asset classes depending on their risk characteristics, such as credit quality, market volatility, and liquidity. U.S. Treasuries are generally treated as the safest and most capital-efficient assets, while equities and private market investments usually attract much heavier capital charges and therefore contribute less efficiently to the insurer’s regulatory capital position.

So Berkshire does have to carry a fairly substantial cash cushion while waiting for that one-in-a-hundred-year event. How big is the cushion exactly? It's most likely up to the management to decide. Berkshire could probably deploy all cash to equities, and even after applying heavy risk weights, would probably still have enough capital to meet regulatory requirements. But it would basically mean that in a large catastrophe, the insurer would have to liquidate investments at fire-sale prices.

At the end of FY2025, Berkshire had $976 billion of assets within the insurance division, including $370 billion of cash and cash equivalents, while the overall insurance liabilities were $306 billion. Only $211 billion was directly related to insurance operations.

Providing the whole c. $200 billion in cash equivalents would be incredibly conservative already, given that the overall equity base of the broader insurance balance sheet is $673 billion. I do believe that a cash level of $120 billion, equivalent to unpaid policy losses, would be enough of a cash cushion.

We would treat this minimum cash cushion as a "cost to play" in the insurance business, while assuming that the remaining $250 billion can be treated as an asset that could be redeployed via acquisitions or buybacks.

Sum-of-the-parts valuation of Berkshire Hathaway

Berkshire Hathaway at the end of FY2025 carried in total about $580 billion worth of investment assets on top of its operating businesses.

The value of operating businesses of Berkshire Hathaway is about $670 billion, while the value of financial investments is close to $584 billion. This puts the overall value of Berkshire at about $1,255 billion. The current market value of the conglomerate is about $1,000 billion.

Large, broadly diversified conglomerates often trade at a discount to the sum of the parts value. However, an argument could be made that Berkshire does not deserve to trade at this discount due to excellent capital stewardship over many decades.

The problem of growth

Berkshire Hathaway is one of the largest companies in the world, and it would therefore be naive to expect to buy it at a large discount to NAV. Most investors buy and hold BRK for ongoing value growth. Having said that, Berkshire's operating earnings have not grown much since the end of 2019, excluding cyclical underwriting profits. BNSF was a relative underperformer, but even other operating divisions have grown earnings at a rather pedestrian rate of c6%.

Equity security holdings did not grow rapidly in value either, as some core holdings, like Apple, were sold down, and no other large-scale bets were made. Cash was the fastest accumulating asset category.
